items.subtopic: Rebecca M. Baron, Beverly W. Baron, Miriam Baron Barshak | ETIOLOGY
items.title: Pattern of Lung Involvement in Bronchiectasis Suggests Etiology
items.content: The pattern of lung involvement in bronchiectasis can provide clues to its etiology. Focal bronchiectasis often results from localized airway obstruction, while diffuse bronchiectasis is associated with systemic or infectious diseases.
keyPoints: Focal bronchiectasis involves localized areas and may result from extrinsic (e.g., lymphadenopathy) or intrinsic (e.g., aspirated foreign body) obstructions.
Diffuse bronchiectasis indicates widespread changes often due to systemic or infectious causes.
Upper lung field involvement is common in CF and post-radiation fibrosis.
Lower lung fields are affected by chronic aspiration, end-stage fibrotic lung disease, or recurrent immunodeficiency-associated infections.
Midlung field bronchiectasis can result from NTM infection (e.g., MAC) or congenital dyskinetic cilia syndrome.
Central airway involvement is seen in ABPA and cartilage deficiency syndromes like Mounier-Kuhn.
items.reference: Bronchiectasis · Rebecca M. Baron, Beverly W. Baron, Miriam Baron Barshak | ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Rebecca M. Baron, Beverly W. Baron, Miriam Baron Barshak | ETIOLOGY
items.title: Idiopathic Bronchiectasis: A Common Diagnostic Challenge
items.content: A significant proportion of bronchiectasis cases remain idiopathic despite thorough evaluation. Identifying disease subphenotypes may improve treatment strategies.
keyPoints: 25–50% of patients with bronchiectasis have an unknown etiology, termed idiopathic bronchiectasis.
There is a need to define disease subphenotypes for better clinical trial targeting and treatment.
items.reference: Bronchiectasis · Rebecca M. Baron, Beverly W. Baron, Miriam Baron Barshak | ETIOLOGY

items.title: Epidemiological Trends in Bronchiectasis and Underlying Etiologies
items.content: The prevalence of bronchiectasis is increasing, with variations based on etiology. Cystic fibrosis (CF) often leads to bronchiectasis in late adolescence or early adulthood, while Mycobacterium avium complex (MAC) infection typically affects nonsmoking women over 50. Bronchiectasis incidence rises with age and is more common among women. It frequently coexists with COPD or asthma.
keyPoints: Bronchiectasis prevalence is increasing in the U.S.
CF-related bronchiectasis often presents in late adolescence to early adulthood
MAC infection-associated bronchiectasis typically affects nonsmoking women >50 years
Incidence of bronchiectasis increases with age and is more common among women
Bronchiectasis frequently co-diagnosed with COPD or asthma

items.title: Pathogenesis of Bronchiectasis: The Vicious Cycle Hypothesis
items.content: The pathogenesis of infectious bronchiectasis is often explained by the vicious cycle hypothesis, where susceptibility to infection and poor mucociliary clearance lead to microbial colonization. Organisms like Pseudomonas aeruginosa can colonize damaged airways and evade host defenses. Impaired mucociliary clearance may result from conditions such as CF or dyskinetic cilia syndrome, or from severe infections like pneumonia caused by Bordetella pertussis or Mycoplasma pneumoniae.
keyPoints: Vicious cycle hypothesis explains infectious bronchiectasis
Susceptibility to infection and poor mucociliary clearance lead to microbial colonization
Pseudomonas aeruginosa is adept at colonizing damaged airways
Impaired mucociliary clearance can result from CF, dyskinetic cilia syndrome, or severe infections like pneumonia caused by Bordetella pertussis or Mycoplasma pneumoniae

items.subtopic: Rebecca M. Baron, Beverly W. Baron, Miriam Baron Barshak | ETIOLOGY
items.question: Which lung field involvement is most commonly associated with bronchiectasis due to cystic fibrosis?
items.explanation: Bronchiectasis in CF typically shows more pronounced involvement of the upper lung fields.
items.reference: Bronchiectasis · Rebecca M. Baron, Beverly W. Baron, Miriam Baron Barshak | ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Rebecca M. Baron, Beverly W. Baron, Miriam Baron Barshak | ETIOLOGY
items.question: What is a common cause of bronchiectasis with predominant lower lung field involvement?
items.explanation: Lower lung field bronchiectasis often results from chronic recurrent aspiration, such as due to esophageal motility disorders.
items.reference: Bronchiectasis · Rebecca M. Baron, Beverly W. Baron, Miriam Baron Barshak | ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Rebecca M. Baron, Beverly W. Baron, Miriam Baron Barshak | ETIOLOGY
items.question: Which condition is associated with central airway-predominant bronchiectasis?
items.explanation: Central airway-predominant bronchiectasis is associated with allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis.
items.reference: Bronchiectasis · Rebecca M. Baron, Beverly W. Baron, Miriam Baron Barshak | ETIOLOGY

items.question: Which population is classically affected by bronchiectasis resulting from MAC infection?
options: Adults in their thirties and forties
items.explanation: Bronchiectasis resulting from MAC infection classically affects nonsmoking women >50 years of age.

items.question: What is a common co-diagnosis with bronchiectasis?
items.explanation: Bronchiectasis may frequently be co-diagnosed with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) or asthma.

items.question: What is a suggested predisposing factor for the development of bronchiectasis in developing nations?
items.explanation: It has been suggested that the high incidence of malnutrition may predispose to immune dysfunction and development of bronchiectasis.

items.subtopic: Rebecca M. Baron, Beverly W. Baron, Miriam Baron Barshak | ETIOLOGY
items.statement: Bronchiectasis can arise from both infectious and noninfectious causes.
items.explanation: The passage states that bronchiectasis can have infectious or noninfectious etiologies.
items.reference: Bronchiectasis · Rebecca M. Baron, Beverly W. Baron, Miriam Baron Barshak | ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Rebecca M. Baron, Beverly W. Baron, Miriam Baron Barshak | ETIOLOGY
items.statement: Focal bronchiectasis is characterized by widespread changes throughout the lung.
items.explanation: Focal bronchiectasis refers to localized changes in a specific area of the lung.
items.reference: Bronchiectasis · Rebecca M. Baron, Beverly W. Baron, Miriam Baron Barshak | ETIOLOGY

items.statement: Bronchiectasis is more common among men than women.
items.explanation: Bronchiectasis is more common among women than among men.

items.statement: In areas where tuberculosis is prevalent, bronchiectasis can occur as a sequela of granulomatous infection.
items.explanation: Bronchiectasis more frequently occurs as a sequela of granulomatous infection in areas where tuberculosis is prevalent.

items.subtopic: Rebecca M. Baron, Beverly W. Baron, Miriam Baron Barshak | ETIOLOGY
items.assertion: Bronchiectasis resulting from nontuberculous mycobacteria often affects the midlung fields.
items.reason: Nontuberculous mycobacterial infections, such as those by Mycobacterium avium-intracellular complex, preferentially affect the midlung fields.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: The incidence of bronchiectasis increases with age.
items.reason: Bronchiectasis may frequently be co-diagnosed with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) or asthma.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason provided does not explain why the incidence of bronchiectasis increases with age.
